headline: The risk we flagged just happened: the Hormuz truce collapsed over the weekend — Iran shut the strait and struck Gulf states — so Friday's calm close (oil $71, VIX 15, Sensex +1%) now faces a Monday repricing, with Bitcoin's -0.5% weekend slip the only live tell.
market_story: Markets closed Friday in a hopeful mood — and then the weekend turned. On Friday the 'war premium' had drained out of oil (US crude WTI ended near $71, its RSI a deeply-oversold ~38), Wall Street's fear gauge (the VIX, which rises when investors expect turbulence) sat at a sleepy 15, US indices ticked to fresh highs and India's Sensex rallied a second day (+1.08% to 77,569). The whole board was priced for de-escalation. Overnight, that reversed hard: Iran fired on a container ship, declared the Strait of Hormuz — the sea gate for a fifth of the world's oil — closed 'until further notice,' and struck US bases in Qatar, Jordan, Kuwait, Oman and the UAE, while the US hit ~140 Iranian targets. Here's the catch for a beginner: stock and oil markets are shut on weekends, so none of this is in Friday's numbers yet. The only market that trades 24/7 — crypto — gives the one live read: Bitcoin slipped ~0.5% to $63,812 as the risk-off mood built. Expect oil to gap higher when trading reopens Monday, which would pressure the rupee (India imports ~85% of its oil), lift gold and the VIX, and test India's rally — even though foreigners had just turned net buyers (₹15,157 crore into Indian stocks in July, ending four months of selling). For the user's book: Reliance (an energy/refining major) is most directly cross-exposed to the oil swing; Nvidia and Apple are high-beta US names that fall fastest in a risk-off gap despite strong trends; Bitcoin is already sliding in a downtrend. The lesson underneath: a calm Friday close ahead of a stormy weekend tells you little — the gap between the last price and the new reality is exactly where Monday's move comes from.

board.name: S&P 500
board.read: US large-caps closed Friday at a fresh high as the war premium faded — but that was BEFORE the weekend re-escalation, so it's a stale calm.

board.name: Nasdaq
board.read: Tech held its gains Friday on the AI-chip revival; a Monday risk-off gap would hit high-beta names first.

board.name: Dow Jones
board.read: Blue chips edged up in a low-drama Friday session — the last print before the Hormuz shock lands.

board.name: VIX (fear gauge)
board.read: The fear gauge closed at ~15 — near-complacency, exactly the calm that can spike hard when a weekend shock reopens trading.

board.name: Nifty 50
board.read: India's benchmark closed Friday above 24,000, but faces a Monday gap risk as oil re-spikes over the weekend.

board.name: Sensex
board.read: Up 828 points Friday, yet still ~0.25% lower for the week — the rally only clawed back part of the earlier slump.

board.name: Bank Nifty
board.read: Banks led Friday on calmer oil; a fresh oil spike and sticky inflation would flip that tailwind to a headwind.

board.name: Bitcoin
board.read: The one market open all weekend: BTC slipped to ~$63.8k as risk-off built — still below its 50- and 200-day averages, a downtrend intact.

board.name: Ethereum
board.read: Barely moved over the weekend, sitting between its key averages — a fragile, transitional tape like the broad crypto complex.

board.name: Gold
board.read: Eased Friday as panic faded — but a classic safe-haven that typically bids up first when a war shock reopens markets.

board.name: Crude Oil (WTI)
board.read: The number that matters most: closed Friday at ~$71 (RSI ~38, oversold) — but a DECLARED Hormuz closure over the weekend points to a sharp Monday gap up.

board.name: USD/INR
board.read: The rupee firmed Friday on cheap oil; a Monday crude spike would pressure it again (more rupees per dollar) — India's key oil-shock channel.

themes.note: The weekend Hormuz closure should gap crude higher Monday, reversing Friday's ~$71 calm — a headwind for oil-importers (airlines, paints, logistics, oil-marketing) and a cross-current for Reliance's refining.
Gold, the dollar, bonds and the VIX are the classic beneficiaries of a war shock; Friday's calm (VIX 15, gold easing) is likely to reverse when markets reopen to the escalation.
Banks, autos and real estate lose the 'calmer oil, firmer rupee' tailwind if crude spikes and June CPI (seen ~4.2%) keeps the RBI cautious.
Nvidia, the AI-chip complex and Bitcoin are the fastest to fall in a risk-off gap; BTC already slipped over the weekend and stays in a downtrend.

what_to_watch.title: Hormuz shut — oil set to gap up on Monday
key_points: Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed 'until further notice'
Iran struck Gulf states; US hit ~140 Iranian targets
WTI closed Friday ~$71 (RSI ~38) — before any of this
A declared closure threatens real supply, not just sentiment
what_to_watch.gist: The exact risk we flagged as #1 yesterday — 'any fresh Hormuz incident would snap the oil war premium back' — has happened. Over the weekend Iran fired on a ship, declared the Strait of Hormuz (the sea gate for a fifth of the world's oil) closed, and traded strikes with the US across the Gulf. US crude closed Friday at about $71 a barrel, its RSI (a 0–100 momentum gauge) near 38, meaning 'oversold' — but that was before the escalation, so the number is stale.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Oil is the master variable for this whole market: it drives inflation, the rupee and whether India's rally holds. A 'war premium' (extra price paid to insure against a supply cut) deflates on calm and inflates fast when a feared cutoff becomes real. A DECLARED strait closure is a real threat to barrels, not just tension — so unlike the earlier scare, this can push oil up and keep it there.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For an oil-importer like India, the difference between ~$71 and a spike above $85 is the difference between a relief rally and a fresh sell-off. Expect crude to gap up when it reopens Monday; watch whether it holds the spike or fades as it did last week.
what_to_watch.watch: Monday's oil open — whether Brent/WTI blow past $80 and hold — and whether Hormuz traffic actually halts.

key_terms.definition: Extra money buyers pay for oil to insure against conflict cutting supply — it inflates fast when a feared cutoff, like a strait closure, actually happens.
The Relative Strength Index measures momentum from 0–100; below ~30 is 'oversold' (sold hard, often due a bounce), above ~70 'overbought'.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: News that breaks while markets are shut doesn't vanish — it stores up for the reopen. A declared supply cutoff is a different animal from a scare, because what actually moves oil is lost barrels, not just fear.

what_to_watch.title: India braces for a Monday gap — even as foreigners just turned buyers
key_points: Sensex closed Friday +828 (+1.08%) at 77,569; Nifty +1.02% at 24,207
Both still slipped ~0.25% for the week
FPIs turned net buyers in July: ~₹15,157 crore, ending 4 months of selling
A weekend oil shock now threatens the improving mood
what_to_watch.gist: India's market had genuinely good news to end the week: not only did the Sensex rally a second day, but foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) reversed a four-month selling streak, pouring roughly ₹15,157 crore into Indian equities in July as the rupee steadied. Yet both indices still finished the week about 0.25% lower, and all of that upbeat set-up now meets a weekend oil shock the market couldn't trade.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: It's a live example of sentiment and reality falling out of sync. Domestic savers (via record monthly SIPs — fixed mutual-fund contributions) plus returning foreigners had rebuilt confidence — but a fresh crude spike arriving over a closed weekend means that confidence is untested against the new bad news. India is the major economy most exposed to Hormuz, so the reprice could be sharp.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For anyone holding Indian stocks, Monday is the test: whether the Nifty holds 24,000, whether the rupee stays firmer than 95.5, and whether the FPI return survives an oil scare. Rate-sensitive banks, autos and real estate are most exposed to a crude-and-inflation double-hit.
what_to_watch.watch: Monday's Nifty open and 24,000 level, the rupee vs 95.5, and whether FPIs keep buying through the escalation.

key_terms.definition: Money foreign investors move into or out of a country's stocks; a swing from selling to buying is a confidence signal, but can reverse fast on a shock.
A systematic investment plan — a fixed monthly sum Indian savers put into mutual funds, giving the market a steady home-grown bid.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: A market's floor is only as strong as its weakest moment tests it — good flows and a firm close mean little until they survive the next shock, so watch how sentiment holds when the news turns, not just when it's calm.

what_to_watch.title: Bitcoin's weekend slip — the market's one live tell
key_points: BTC eased ~0.5% to $63,812 over the weekend; Ether flat at ~$1,795
Crypto is the only market open when stocks and oil are shut
Still below its 50-day (~$65,135) and 200-day (~$73,985) averages
Sits near the bottom of its 52-week range (10th percentile)
what_to_watch.gist: With stocks and oil closed, Bitcoin is the only large market trading through the weekend — so its move is the single live read on how investors feel about the escalation. It slipped about 0.5% to $63,812, a quiet risk-off drift rather than a crash. Zoom out and it's still weak: BTC trades below both its 50-day (~$65,135) and 200-day (~$73,985) averages — a textbook downtrend — near the bottom of its one-year range.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Bitcoin has been trading like a high-risk tech bet, not 'digital gold' — it falls on fear days and bounces on risk-on days, the same rhythm as AI stocks. Its weekend dip hints Monday's stock open could lean risk-off. A bounce inside a downtrend is common; the trend only turns when price reclaims those falling averages.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For a newcomer, treat BTC's weekend move as a sentiment thermometer, not a forecast — and remember flat-to-down near the lows is not a bottom. It needs a close back above ~$65,135 (its 50-day) to signal the downtrend is genuinely turning.
what_to_watch.watch: Whether BTC holds ~$63,640 support or slides further into Monday; a reclaim of ~$65,135 would be the first sign of a turn.

key_terms.definition: A pattern of falling prices where rebounds tend to fade; trading below key moving averages confirms it.
The average price over a set window (e.g. 50 or 200 days); reclaiming it signals a stalled or falling trend may be turning.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: When most markets are closed, watch the one that's open — a 24/7 asset like Bitcoin becomes a real-time gauge of risk appetite, previewing how the closed markets may open.

what_to_watch.title: US jobs cooled — a muddied Fed path now meets an oil shock
key_points: US added only 57,000 jobs in June vs ~115,000 expected
Unemployment fell to 4.2% — but as people quit looking for work
Labour-force participation hit a 50-year low (ex-Covid)
US Fed rate decision due 29 July; officials split on inflation
what_to_watch.gist: America's job engine is cooling: US payrolls rose just 57,000 in June, about half what was expected, and the jobless rate fell to 4.2% for the 'wrong reason' — discouraged workers dropping out, not finding jobs. Normally a soft labour market pushes the Federal Reserve (the US central bank) toward cutting interest rates. But Fed officials are split on whether inflation is beaten, and now a fresh oil spike threatens to reignite it.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: US rates drive the dollar and global risk appetite, which ripple straight into India. A dovish Fed (leaning to cuts) usually weakens the dollar and helps emerging markets like India; but if the Hormuz oil spike lifts inflation, the Fed may hold, keeping the dollar firm and pressuring the rupee. The two forces now pull against each other.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For the user's US and India holdings, the near-term swing factor is which wins — soft jobs (rate-cut friendly, risk-on) or an oil-driven inflation scare (rate-hold, risk-off). Watch the next US CPI print and Fed signals into 29 July.
what_to_watch.watch: The next US CPI inflation reading and Fed commentary ahead of the 29 July decision.

key_terms.definition: The monthly count of US jobs added (excluding farms) — the headline gauge of US economic strength.
Dovish means leaning toward lower rates to support growth; hawkish means leaning toward higher rates to fight inflation.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: A central bank weighs two things at once — jobs and inflation — and an external shock like an oil spike can tip the balance, so the same weak jobs report can be bullish or bearish depending on what oil does next.

what_to_watch.title: India inflation likely topped 4% — and Q1 earnings week begins
key_points: June CPI seen ~4.2% (Mint poll) — first breach of the RBI's 4% target
A weekend oil spike would reload fuel and LPG price pressure
IT and bank Q1 results due this week (Infosys, HCLTech, banks)
SBI Funds Management IPO opens 14 July
what_to_watch.gist: India's retail inflation for June likely rose to about 4.2%, above the Reserve Bank of India's 4% medium-term target for the first time under the revised price index, as costlier fuel and food fed through. A weekend crude spike from Hormuz would reload that pressure (petrol, diesel, LPG cooking gas). Meanwhile Q1 FY27 earnings season broadens this week, with more IT majors and banks reporting.
what_to_watch.why_it_matters: Inflation is the leash on the central bank: the higher it runs, the less room the RBI has to cut rates to support growth. Earnings, meanwhile, decide whether valuations are justified — after TCS's beat set a firm tone, weak results into an oil scare could sour sentiment fast. Both land into the Hormuz uncertainty.
what_to_watch.what_it_means: For rate-sensitive sectors (banks, autos, real estate) and borrowers, sticky inflation means rate relief stays on hold longer. Watch IT and bank results and the official CPI print for the near-term direction of Indian stocks.
what_to_watch.watch: The official June CPI release, Infosys/HCLTech/bank Q1 numbers, and the SBI Funds Management IPO (opens 14 July).

key_terms.definition: The Consumer Price Index tracks how fast the prices households pay are rising; food and fuel are its biggest movers in India.
India's first fiscal quarter — April to June 2026; Indian firms report on an April–March financial year.

what_to_watch.the_lesson: Inflation and earnings are the two anchors under a market: prices need earnings to justify them and low-enough inflation to let the central bank stay supportive — when an oil shock threatens both at once, the risk compounds.

global_summary.us: US indices closed Friday at fresh highs (S&P +0.4%, Nasdaq +0.3%, Dow +0.3%) with the VIX at ~15 — but that calm predates the weekend Hormuz re-escalation, so Monday's open faces a risk-off reprice. June jobs cooled sharply (payrolls +57k) and the Fed (decision 29 July) is split on inflation.
global_summary.india: India rallied a second day Friday (Sensex +828 to 77,569, Nifty +1.02% to 24,207) and FPIs turned net buyers in July (~₹15,157 crore, ending four months of selling) — but both indices still fell ~0.25% for the week, and a weekend oil shock now threatens a Monday gap. India imports ~85% of its oil, making it the most Hormuz-exposed major market.
global_summary.crypto: Bitcoin — the only market open all weekend — slipped ~0.5% to $63,812 as risk-off built, with Ether flat at ~$1,795; both remain below key averages in a longer downtrend.
global_summary.macro: WTI crude closed Friday at ~$71 (RSI ~38, oversold), but Iran's declared Strait of Hormuz closure over the weekend points to a sharp Monday gap up. Gold eased and the dollar stayed firm Friday — classic safe-havens likely to bid up when markets reopen. India's June CPI probably topped the RBI's 4% target.

catalysts_today.event: Oil & Asian equities reprice the weekend Hormuz closure
Strait of Hormuz escalation — any further move re-rates the oil premium
India Q1 results — Infosys, HCLTech, Wipro, banks
SBI Funds Management IPO opens (to 16 July)

favorites.name: Apple Inc.
favorites.whats_new: Unchanged over the weekend at $315 (Friday -0.3%), still pinned just below its 52-week high ($316) — it sits in the 99th percentile of its one-year range, so momentum is strong but it's stretched and short-term overbought, exactly the profile that pulls back first in a risk-off Monday gap.

news.headline: Wall St closed Friday at highs before a weekend Hormuz re-escalation reset the risk backdrop

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~$4.63T) at a rich P/E of 38 (forward 33) — investors are paying up for growth. Revenue growing ~17% a year with ~27% margins; beta ~1.1 means it moves roughly with the market. Dividend yield ~0.3%.
favorites.technicals: A confirmed uptrend (Strong Buy): price ($315) sits well above its 50-day (~$298) and 200-day (~$272) averages, and all 10 moving averages are bullish. But RSI 63 plus Stochastic (96), CCI (100) and Williams %R (all overbought) flash a short-term caution flag right at the highs. First support ~$313, then the 50-day ~$298; first resistance ~$317.
favorites.catalysts: Its own earnings later in the season, the Apple-vs-OpenAI trade-secrets suit, and whether the tech tape holds through a war-driven risk-off.
favorites.risks: Overbought at a 52-week high raises the odds of a pullback; a rich valuation leaves no room for disappointment, and a Monday risk-off gap from the Hormuz shock would hit stretched high-fliers first.

favorites.name: NVIDIA Corp.
favorites.whats_new: Holds Friday's breakout at $211 (unchanged over the weekend) — it had jumped 4% and closed back above its 50-day average (~$209), flipping the trend to a confirmed uptrend (Strong Buy) on SK Hynix's record AI-memory debut. Less oil-exposed than most, but as a high-beta (2.21) risk asset it's still vulnerable to a Monday risk-off gap.

news.headline: AI-chip trade revived on SK Hynix's record $26.5bn Nasdaq debut; Nvidia reclaimed its 50-day average

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~$5.11T) at a P/E of 32 (forward 16, implying strong expected profit growth). Revenue growing ~85% a year with ~63% margins; beta 2.21 makes it far more volatile than the market. Tiny 0.5% dividend.
favorites.technicals: Uptrend regained: price ($211) is above both its 50-day (~$209) and 200-day (~$192) averages, all 10 moving averages bullish and MACD improving. But trend strength is weak (ADX 17) and Stochastic/Williams %R are overbought — expect choppy action. A close back below ~$209 would warn the breakout is failing; first resistance ~$214, first support ~$205.
favorites.catalysts: AI-chip demand read-through from SK Hynix and peers, China chip-access rules, and Nvidia's next earnings.
favorites.risks: The breakout is fresh and trend strength weak, so it could fail; high beta means sharp swings, 'AI fatigue' chatter is building, and a Hormuz-driven risk-off Monday would pressure it despite the strong trend.

favorites.name: Bitcoin
favorites.whats_new: Slipped ~0.5% over the weekend to $63,812 — and as the only market open, that dip is the live tell that the Hormuz escalation has investors leaning risk-off. It stays below its 50- and 200-day averages, so this is still a downtrend, not a turn.

news.headline: Crypto — trading through the weekend — eased as Iran shut Hormuz and struck Gulf states

favorites.fundamentals: Roughly $1.28T in total value. No cash flows or earnings — its price is driven by risk appetite, interest-rate expectations and flows into/out of spot Bitcoin ETFs.
favorites.technicals: Downtrend intact: price (~$63,812) is below both its 50-day (~$65,135) and 200-day (~$73,985) averages and sits near the bottom of its 52-week range (10th percentile). Trend strength is strong (ADX 25) and pointing down, so down-moves have tended to persist; RSI 52 is neutral and MACD is improving. It needs a close above ~$65,135 to signal a real turn; first support ~$63,640.
favorites.catalysts: How the Hormuz risk-off plays out, spot-Bitcoin-ETF flows, and the tone of the 29 July Fed decision.
favorites.risks: A confirmed downtrend near one-year lows; it trades like a high-beta risk asset, so a deepening war shock and continued ETF outflows would cap rebounds and could push it toward its 52-week low (~$58,559).

favorites.name: Reliance Industries
favorites.whats_new: Closed Friday +2.2% at ₹1,308 with the India rally, rating nudged up to Neutral (from Strong Sell) — but it's the favorite most directly in the crossfire of the weekend oil shock. As an energy/refining major, a Hormuz-driven crude spike cuts both ways (helps some segments, squeezes refining and demand), and Friday's bounce could reverse if India gaps down Monday.

news.headline: India rallied Friday as oil eased — but a weekend Hormuz closure now threatens a Monday reprice

favorites.fundamentals: Mega-cap (~₹17.7T) in energy, at a moderate P/E of 22 (forward 18). Revenue growing ~12% a year with thin ~8% margins (typical of refining); beta 0.18 makes it much steadier than the market. Dividend yield ~0.5%.
favorites.technicals: Downtrend, though improving: price (₹1,308) is below its 50-day (~₹1,332) and 200-day (~₹1,416) averages and sits in the 15th percentile of its 52-week range. RSI 49 is neutral and MACD has turned positive; Friday's pop lifted it above its 10- and 30-day averages. First resistance ~₹1,317, then the 50-day ~₹1,332; first support ~₹1,293.
favorites.catalysts: Crude-price direction (refining margins), any energy/retail/Jio updates, and its own Q1 results later in the season.
favorites.risks: Weak technical structure near 52-week lows; the bounce needs to reclaim the 50-day (~₹1,332) to mean anything, and a sharp oil spike plus a broad India risk-off Monday could squeeze it lower.

risks: Hormuz shock is now REAL, not a scare: Iran declared the strait closed and struck Gulf states over the weekend — oil should gap up Monday, snapping the war premium back and pressuring Indian equities and the rupee.
Weekend gap risk: stock and oil markets closed Friday calm (oil $71, VIX 15) before the escalation, so Monday's open faces a repricing that isn't in current numbers.
FPI reversal could reverse: foreigners just turned net buyers of India (~₹15,157 cr in July) — a fresh oil shock could send them back to the exits, thinning the bid under the market.

Sticky inflation + muddied Fed: India's June CPI likely topped 4% and a crude spike would reload fuel prices, while a split US Fed (decision 29 July) leaves rate direction uncertain — a headwind for rate-sensitive stocks and crypto.
High-beta downside: Nvidia's breakout is fresh and trend-weak, Apple is overbought at its highs, and Bitcoin is in a downtrend already slipping — all fall fastest in a risk-off Monday.
